topic: A person's car is towed from their own driveway
raw_output:
[story 1]

The morning sun, usually a cheerful presence, felt like a harsh spotlight on my slumped shoulders. I dragged myself out of bed, the sheets clinging uncomfortably to my skin. Every movement felt like lifting lead weights. When I reached the garage, the sight of the tow truck idling near my familiar driveway sent a dull ache through my chest. I just stood there, my gaze fixed on the flashing lights, a heavy sigh escaping my lips. "What in the world?" I mumbled, the sound raspy. The driver, a man with a perpetually unimpressed expression, just pointed toward the vehicle. I didn't have the energy to argue, only a profound sense of deflation washing over me.

[story 2]

I stared at the notification on my phone, the screen illuminating my face with a sickly blue light. Another roadside inconvenience. I rubbed my eyes, the lids feeling gritty and heavy. The thought of dealing with the bureaucracy, the endless calls, made my jaw clench. When the tow truck arrived, the sudden intrusion of noise and activity was too much. I managed a weak nod to the operator, my voice barely a whisper. "It's just… everything," I managed, the words tasting dry. I shuffled toward the curb, every step requiring a conscious effort against a deep-seated lethargy that seemed to anchor me to the spot.

[story 3]

The key fob felt impossibly heavy in my palm. I had intended to be quick, to just get the car moving before the day truly began. Instead, I found myself moving with agonizing slowness, each muscle protesting the exertion. When the tow truck pulled up, the sudden reality of the situation hit me like a physical blow. I leaned against the doorframe, closing my eyes for a brief, desperate moment. The sheer effort of simply existing felt monumental. "This is just… too much today," I murmured to the empty air, the sound thin and defeated. I watched the crew begin their work, feeling utterly depleted.
